text.title: Poetry Setup and Dependency Management Rules
text.summary: Project initialization, pyproject.toml structure, dependency group rules, and lockfile policy.

rule.statement: Always commit poetry.lock. There is no situation where a service or library should omit it from version control.
rule.rationale: Without the lock, two agents on different machines resolve different transitive dependency trees, producing non-reproducible builds and subtle environment bugs.

rule.statement: Run poetry install --sync --no-interaction in CI. The --sync flag removes packages that left the lockfile, preventing zombie dependencies.
rule.rationale: Without --sync, deleted dependencies accumulate in CI environments across runs and cause false-positive test passes.

rule.statement: Never run poetry update without arguments in a shared repo. It rewrites the entire lockfile, overwriting another developer's or agent's staged resolver result. Use poetry update <package> to bump a single package.
rule.rationale: Full lockfile rewrites are destructive to concurrent work and produce unreviewed dependency changes.

section.title: Dependency Groups

rule.statement: Split dependencies into groups: main, dev, test, docs. Mark docs as optional = true so production installs don't pull mkdocs. Keep production deps minimal.
rule.rationale: Smaller production dependency trees mean smaller Docker images, fewer CVE surface area, and faster pip install in the final stage.

example.code:
# Production poetry add fastapi uvicorn sqlalchemy
# Dev tools poetry add --group dev ruff mypy black pytest
# Optional docs poetry add --group docs --optional mkdocs mkdocs-material
# Install specific groups in CI poetry install --only main poetry install --with dev,test

section.reference: Full pyproject.toml with groups and tool configs

rule.statement: Use tight Python version constraints (python = "^3.11", not ">=3.8"). Broad ranges slow the solver and produce hostile error messages for conflicting extras.
rule.rationale: The Poetry solver has quadratic complexity in constraint width. Tight Python pins halve the search space.

rule.statement: Before any poetry add or poetry remove, run poetry check --lock. If the lockfile is dirty, resolve that first before adding new dependencies.
rule.rationale: Adding a package when the lockfile is dirty silently merges the dirty state into the new lock, creating an unreviewed dependency change.

list.item: poetry new my-project — create new project with standard layout
poetry init — initialize in existing directory
poetry install --sync --no-interaction -- install from lock, prune zombies
poetry add --group dev pytest ruff -- add dev dependency
poetry update <package> -- bump single package only
poetry export -f requirements.txt --output requirements.txt -- export for Docker
poetry check --lock -- verify lockfile is current
poetry env info --path -- show virtual environment path
